title: Quiz: Quantum Matrix Operations
description: Test your understanding of block-encoding, quantum matrix multiplication, and singular value transformation

questions.question: What is block-encoding in quantum linear algebra?

options.text: Encoding classical data into quantum bits
Embedding a non-unitary matrix as a block in a larger unitary
Dividing a matrix into smaller blocks for parallel processing
Converting sparse matrices to dense format

questions.explanation: Block-encoding embeds a matrix A (which may not be unitary) into the upper-left block of a larger unitary U. This allows quantum computers to manipulate non-unitary matrices through unitary operations.

questions.question: If U block-encodes A/α, how do you extract A|b⟩ from the circuit output?

options.text: Measure all qubits and classical post-process
Post-select on ancilla qubits being in |0⟩ state
Apply inverse QFT to the output
Repeat measurements until convergence

questions.explanation: When applying the block-encoding U to |0⟩|b⟩, the state becomes α^(-1)|0⟩(A|b⟩) + |⊥⟩. Post-selecting on ancilla |0⟩ projects onto the desired result A|b⟩/α.

questions.question: What happens to the normalization factor when multiplying two block-encoded matrices?

options.text: It stays the same
It adds: α_total = α_A + α_B
It multiplies: α_total = α_A × α_B
It is the maximum: α_total = max(α_A, α_B)

questions.explanation: When composing block-encodings of A/α_A and B/α_B, the result block-encodes AB/(α_A × α_B). The normalization factors multiply, which can lead to exponentially small success probabilities for long chains of operations.

questions.question: What does Quantum Singular Value Transformation (QSVT) enable?

options.text: Only matrix inversion
Only eigenvalue extraction
Applying arbitrary polynomial functions to singular values
Converting classical algorithms to quantum

questions.explanation: QSVT can apply any polynomial function f to the singular values of a matrix: if A = ΣσᵢUᵢVᵢ†, QSVT produces f(A) = Σf(σᵢ)UᵢVᵢ†. This unifies many quantum algorithms including HHL, amplitude amplification, and more.

questions.question: Block-encoding always requires ancilla qubits.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! Block-encoding embeds an n×n matrix into a 2n×2n (or larger) unitary. The additional rows/columns correspond to ancilla qubits needed to make the overall operation unitary.

questions.question: How does QSVT complexity for matrix inversion compare to HHL?

options.text: QSVT is always slower
QSVT: O(κ log(1/ε)) vs HHL: O(κ²/ε)
They have identical complexity
HHL is always faster for any matrix

questions.explanation: QSVT achieves O(κ log(1/ε)) complexity for matrix inversion, improving over HHL's O(κ²/ε). The improvement comes from better polynomial approximations and avoiding repeated phase estimation.

questions.question: Which matrix property most affects quantum matrix operation efficiency?

options.text: Whether entries are real or complex
Sparsity and condition number
Whether the matrix is square
The sign of eigenvalues

questions.explanation: Sparsity s affects query complexity (fewer queries for sparse matrices), while condition number κ affects both the required precision and success probability. Both appear polynomially in quantum complexity bounds.

questions.question: Which statements about quantum matrix operations are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Quantum speedup is exponential in matrix dimension for sparse matrices
Loading classical data into quantum states can negate speedup
Success probability decreases with condition number
Quantum operations output the full matrix inverse

questions.explanation: a) Correct - O(polylog n) vs O(n²) for matrix-vector products on sparse matrices. b) Correct - state preparation can be O(n), negating speedup. c) Correct - success probability scales as O(1/κ²). d) Incorrect - quantum operations output quantum states, not classical matrices.
